nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on fine sand and silty-sand bottoms in shallow tropical and subtropical waters — reef lagoons, sheltered reef sand-flats, sandy patches between coral heads, and seagrass margins. One of the most widely distributed sand-bed sea stars in the Indo-West Pacific.

decorPreferences: deep sand bed (7+ cm) of fine aragonite or oolitic sand
established live rock to seed microfauna
minimal coarse rubble that could trap arms

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on infaunal microcrustaceans (copepods, amphipods), polychaete worms, small bivalves, and detrital organic matter sifted from the sand bed. Has been documented opportunistically consuming small snails, juvenile bivalves, and other small sea stars, so it should not be assumed to be a benign detritivore. Generally advised to be target-fed small pieces of meaty frozen food (mysis, clam, chopped shrimp) placed near the buried specimen once microfauna populations decline, as natural sand-bed prey is typically exhausted within months in home aquaria.

goodWith: soft corals and LPS placed on rockwork (off the sand)
wrasses that do not bury in sand
avoidWith: sand-sleeping wrasses that compete for the same infauna
other sand-sifting sea stars in tanks under ~500 L

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by John Martin PERRY, 1600x1067. Live Archaster typicus in a shallow tidepool showing the diagnostic grey-and-black mottled dorsal pattern, tapered arms, and prominent marginal plates; sharp, whole animal in natural habitat.
Photo by coenobita, 2048x1536. Live specimen in situ on a sandy bottom underwater, sand-camouflaged coloration and marginal spine combs clearly visible; well represents its sand-sifting habitat.
Photo by Portioid, 1600x900. Several live specimens held over sand showing the greyish-green dorsal surface and marginal plates; multiple individuals, slightly stylized processing.

summary: The Sand-Sifting Sea Star (Archaster typicus) is among the most widely traded sea stars in the reef hobby, burrowing through fine sand to consume microfauna and detritus. It is generally considered more reef-safe than the similar Astropecten polyacanthus, but is notorious for slowly starving in tanks where the sand bed cannot sustain it.
careNotes:
Tank-size and mature-tank caveat (high importance): The Sand-Sifting Sea Star is widely reported to deplete the deep-sand-bed microfauna of smaller systems and then slowly starve over weeks to months, often unnoticed because it remains buried. The hobby consensus is approximately 75 US gallons (~280-300 L) per specimen at minimum, in a tank that has been established for at least 6 months so that copepod, amphipod, polychaete, and small-bivalve populations are well seeded. Even at that floor, supplemental target-feeding of meaty frozen foods (mysis, chopped clam, finely cut silversides) is generally advised once natural infauna shows signs of decline. A starved specimen that dies buried in the substrate can release a significant ammonia spike and crash the tank — careful observation of weekly emergence and feeding behavior is important.

Reef-safe with caveats: The Sand-Sifting Sea Star is generally considered reef-safe in the sense that it does not target corals; however it is NOT a benign detritivore. It is documented to consume small snails, juvenile bivalves (including ornamental clams settling near the sand), small ornamental sea stars (e.g. Asterina spp. — sometimes a benefit), and tube worms within its sand-sifting path. It also bulldozes the sand bed mechanically, which can topple unstable rockwork and uproot frags placed directly on substrate; ensure rock structures are seated on the glass, not on sand.

Sand bed: A fine aragonite or oolitic sand bed of at least 7-8 cm (3 inches) over a broad footprint is required for natural feeding behavior. Coarse crushed coral and large grain sizes have been reported to abrade the tube feet and to trap the animal. A larger horizontal sand footprint matters more than total tank volume; long, low aquaria suit this species better than tall narrow ones.

Water chemistry: Like most echinoderms, A. typicus is very intolerant of rapid changes in salinity, pH, and dissolved oxygen, and is acutely sensitive to copper-based medications and most invertebrate-toxic treatments. Drip-acclimate over 1-2 hours and never expose the specimen to air during transfer. Maintain stable specific gravity (1.023-1.025), pH 8.1-8.4, and avoid sudden water-change shocks.

Behavior: Largely nocturnal; spends most daylight hours partially or fully buried, emerging at night to feed across the sand. A specimen that has not emerged in over a week, or that floats to the surface, is typically already deceased or moribund — remove promptly to prevent ammonia release.

Trade species identity: The 'Sand-Sifting Sea Star' label is applied to several species in the trade — most commonly Archaster typicus but historically also Astropecten polyacanthus and other Astropecten spp. Husbandry is broadly similar across these species; however A. typicus is generally regarded as more reef-safe, being less likely to consume ornamental snails, juvenile clams, or other small ornamental invertebrates. If an arriving specimen has very long, slender arms with prominent paxillae (the small raised plates along the upper margin) and a more steeply tapered arm tip, it may be an Astropecten rather than an Archaster; both species respond well to the same husbandry approach.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Sand Sifting Sea Star
primary.notes: Used for temperature (72-78°F), pH (8.1-8.4), specific gravity (1.023-1.025), max size (~8 inches), care level (easy), reef-safe, peaceful temperament, native range (Indo-Pacific), copper-sensitivity, and the don't-house-with-puffers compatibility note.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Sand Sifting Sea Star Species Profile
additional.notes: Substituted in place of Reef2Reef Wiki (which returned HTTP 403). Cross-confirmed temperature, pH, specific gravity. Provided 75-gallon minimum tank size, 3-inch+ minimum sand bed depth, and the explicit microfauna-depletion-in-undersized-tanks warning. Adult-size reported as 10 inches (vs LiveAquaria's 8 inches) — averaged to ~20-25 cm armspread range, also consistent with Wikipedia's 20 cm armspread figure.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Archaster typicus
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Archasteridae, order Valvatida), Indo-West Pacific distribution (East Africa to the central Pacific, north to Japan, south to Australia), shallow-water depth range (intertidal to ~60 m), and arm structure. Replaced an earlier Wikipedia reference to Astropecten polyacanthus when the species identity of this entry was corrected to Archaster typicus per the corpus-completeness gap analysis (2026-06-14).

additional.name: Wikipedia – Astropecten polyacanthus (historical conflation reference)
additional.notes: Retained as a reference for the trade-name conflation paragraph in careNotes. Astropecten polyacanthus is the species this entry was originally documented as before the 2026-06-14 species-identity correction; it remains a real species sold under the same 'Sand-Sifting Sea Star' trade name and is documented here as a recognised confusion source rather than as the entry's primary species.

additional.name: FishkeepingWorld – Sand Sifting Starfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Third source used because of the high-stakes tank-size and starvation guidance. Confirmed 75-gallon minimum, kH 8-12 dKH, lifespan 3-5 years, mature-tank requirement, and the burrow-and-starve-then-release-ammonia failure mode that is widely reported but rarely quantified.
